title: What’s my counteroffer rate, and how do I keep it low?
bodyHtml:
Counteroffers are very critical to customer satisfaction and require extra effort from you, which is why you need to avoid them whenever possible. It’s recommended that you counteroffer in only 20% to 25% of received orders.

 

 

How is my Counteroffer Rate calculated?

The Counteroffer Rate measures the percentage of orders that you received and for which you made a counteroffer (CO) to the customer.

This is how the Counteroffer rate is computed: We count all the CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days. We add an estimation of still-undeclared CO for your orders pending processing or pending a customer action by applying your raw CO rate* to them. We divide by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.

The Counteroffer rate is calculated every day.

 

*raw CO rate is the old definition of the CO rate, computed as: Number of CO you made for the orders received in the last 30 days, divided by the number of orders you received in the last 30 days.

 

 

When NOT to make a counteroffer?

Counteroffers must be justified, fair in value, and can only be made rarely. Therefore, there are various cases in which counteroffer is not permitted.

 

Non-legitimate CO reason

Based on the customer declaration for their device, and the grade associated, you have reasons of CO not allowed for each device grade (e.g. If the customer has declared their phone broken, you cannot make a CO reporting it is broken).

 

Access here the full list of allowed – and not allowed – CO reasons for each grade and each product category:


Click here to access the list in English.

Click here to access the list in German.

Click here to access the list in Spanish.

 

Learn more about grading guidelines.

 

Notably, you cannot make a counteroffer for:

 

  • Grade 0 items (Functional - Flawless) with few faint signs of wear
  • Grade 1 items (Functional - Very good) with deep scratches
  • Grade 5 items (Not functional — broken parts with cracks)
  • Grade 4 items (Not functional — broken parts with scratches) except in cases of broken screens, broken back panels, and / or broken side panels
  • Unofficial parts as items sold by Back Market customers have had previous lives and may have been refurbished
  • Missing accessories if they're not mandatory
  • Accessories that are not broken

For EU refurbishers, a non-European item isn’t a sufficient reason to refuse it or to make a counteroffer

 

Non-spottable issues

Do not make a counteroffer for non-spottable issues. A non-spottable issue is a technical issue that can't be spotted by a person without special skills in device repair. Non-spottable issues can be present on all features of an item.

Here are just a few examples of non-spottable issues:

 

  • False contact at the charging connector
  • Weak vibration intensity
  • Muffled microphone audio
  • Small black spot on the rear camera

How to make a counteroffer?

When you have no other possibility, here’s our guideline to make a counteroffer:

 

Set a reasonable price for your counteroffer

Make sure the counteroffer price is consistent with:

 

  • Initial price
  • Standard industry costs to repair the damage
  • Your price for the new grade of the item, or Back Market prices for new grade of the item


ℹ️ Remember that likeliness of the customer to accept the counteroffer is heavily dependent on the amount of your counteroffer

 

Justify it clearly and accurately

Counteroffers must be handled with care and tact. You must send the Back Market customer as much information as possible, so they fully understand why the counter-offered amount is different from the original amount.

When making a counteroffer, you must:

 

  • Select the right reason(s) that justify the counteroffer among the list provided

⚠️ All reasons are displayed from your Back Office. Be careful on choosing  one/several reasons which are allowed for the grade of the device you received

 

  • Add at least one piece of evidence (photo, video)
    • If the counteroffer is linked to the appearance of the item, include high-quality photos of all damage that justifies a lower appearance grade
    • If the counteroffer is linked to the functional condition of the item, include evidence of each functional problem such as a testing software report or video
  • Add comments so that customers understand why they are counteroffered at a lower price. 

Here are our top recommendations for counteroffer evidence:

Set up a place where you can take clear, high-quality photos:

 

  • Take pictures against a white background
  • Use consistent, uniform lighting on all of your pictures (see this tool for suggested lighting) 
  • Use a pointer to point out any defects to make your picture as self-explanatory as possible

Save time when communicating pieces of evidence between your quality and care teams:

 

  • Update all proof to the cloud so that your care team can easily retrieve evidence when chatting with Back Market customers (use a cloud-based solution like Google Drive or OneDrive)
  • Organize all evidence by order number so that customer care teams can easily find the proof

Choose well-known software for quality testing:

 

  • We recommend Blancco, PhoneCheck, and NSYS Group 
  • If you use other software, let us know, and we’ll update our recommendations

ℹ️ Remember that likeliness of the customer to accept the counteroffer is impacted by the clarity of the counteroffer.

 

Note that Back Market may pay the Back Market customer the initial price if you don't submit sufficient evidence to justify a counteroffer or make an non-legitimate counter offer.

 

 

What happens if my Counteroffer rate is too high?

The higher your counteroffer rate is, the higher the prices for sourcing will be for you. Indeed, since the launch of the Trade-in BackBox as of June 2025, you are rewarded for having a lower counteroffer rate than your competitors by being able to access the BackBox for a lower price. Learn more about the Trade-in BackBox.


Also, if your Counteroffer Rate is too high, you’ll enter a “monitoring period" where you have the opportunity to improve your rate. If your rate doesn’t improve enough, your daily Trade-in limit will be decreased. If the issue persists, you won’t be able to use the Trade-in service.

​​​​​​​Learn more about Counteroffer Rate thresholds and how the monitoring period works.

 

 

How do I keep my Counteroffer Rate low?

Check your Back Office frequently to monitor your counteroffer rate


Optimize your offer

 

  • Set your listing price with the updated cosmetic and functional definitions in mind. (See 2. and 3.)
  • The older the model, the lower the chance you have of receiving devices without scratches.
  • Diversify your inventory by buying multiple cosmetic and functional grades.
  • Optimize your Trade-in offer prices by analyzing the profitability of your recent Trade-in orders.


Apply our operational recommendations

  • Read the grading guidelines carefully, print and share with your teams. (Print pages with 🖨️ in the lower right corner). Learn more and access grading guidelines.
  • Don't be too "picky" about cosmetic or low-repair-cost functional issues.
  • Remember, there is no link between Trade-in and Sales grading.


Remember that Trade-in grading isn't the same as the sales appearance grades.
 

 

How should I monitor my performance indicators?

You can check the progress of your Counteroffer Rate and other performance indicators in your Trade-in insights dashboard. You can access these from your Sales Back Office, then “Insights” > “Trade-in”.


If you have any questions, contact your Back Market point of contact or reach out via the Seller Support Center.
